preamble: The diphthong /au/, characterized by an open central or back unrounded vowel followed by a close back rounded vowel, has undergone a notable phonetic transformation to /oi/ in the historical development of certain languages. This shift involves a complex re-articulation of both elements of the diphthong, resulting in a sound that begins with a mid-to-open-mid back rounded vowel and ends with a close front unrounded vowel.
phoneticEffects: The shift from /au/ to /oi/ typically involves the raising and fronting of the initial vowel element (from /a/ to /o/ or /ɔ/) and the fronting and unrounding of the second vowel element (from /u/ to /i/)

examples.note: Some Austronesian languages have shown a shift from **au to oi, presumably via an intermediate *ai stage.

sources: The Romance Languages by Rebecca Posner.
Maluku Linguistic Field Research Report by James T. Collins.
